flashcards.back: A virtual machine is a software-based emulation of a physical computer. It provides an interface identical to the underlying bare hardware, allowing multiple operating systems to run simultaneously on a single physical machine, each believing it has its own dedicated hardware.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors?
flashcards.back:
Type 1 (Bare-metal): Runs directly on hardware, no host OS. Higher performance, used in data centers. Examples: VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen.
Type 2 (Hosted): Runs on top of a host OS as an application. Lower performance, easier to set up. Examples: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation.

Para-virtualization is a technique where the guest OS is modified to be aware it runs in a VM. Instead of executing privileged instructions (which get trapped), the guest makes explicit hypercalls to the hypervisor. This provides better performance but requires source code access to modify the guest OS.

flashcards.front: What are the main benefits of virtual machines?
flashcards.back:
1. Isolation (crash in one VM does not affect others)
2. Server consolidation (multiple VMs on one server)
3. Testing and development (multiple OS environments)
4. Security/sandboxing (run untrusted code safely)
5. Snapshots and recovery
6. Live migration between hosts

flashcards.front: How is JVM different from a system-level VM?
flashcards.back: JVM is an application-level VM that executes Java bytecode, providing platform independence. A system-level VM (VMware, VirtualBox) virtualizes entire hardware, running complete operating systems. JVM virtualizes a runtime environment, not the hardware.
